questions.question: Which statistic is found by summing all the values and dividing by the number of values?

explanations.A: Correct. The mean = (sum of values) / (count of values), the standard average.
explanations.B: The median is the middle of the sorted data, not a sum divided by count.
explanations.C: The mode is the most frequently occurring value, not an average.
explanations.D: The range is max − min, a measure of spread, not center.

questions.question: When people say the 'average' of a dataset, which measure are they usually describing?

explanations.A: The mode is the most common value, which is not what 'average' usually means.
explanations.B: Correct. The everyday 'average' is the arithmetic mean: sum of values divided by how many there are.
explanations.C: The median is the middle value; it coincides with the mean only for symmetric data.
explanations.D: The maximum is the largest single value, not an average of all values.

questions.question: Which measure of center is the middle value once the data is sorted in order?

explanations.A: The mean is the arithmetic average, not the positional middle.
explanations.B: The mode is the most frequent value, not the positional middle.
explanations.C: Correct. After sorting, the median is the middle value (or the average of the two middle values).
explanations.D: The range measures spread (max − min), not the center.

questions.question: Which measure of center depends only on the rank (position) of values, not on their magnitudes?

explanations.A: The mean uses every value's magnitude, so large values shift it.
explanations.B: The range is literally max minus min, so magnitudes matter a great deal.
explanations.C: The variance squares magnitudes of deviations, so it depends heavily on magnitude.
explanations.D: Correct. The median is determined by ordering and selecting the middle rank, so magnitudes of extremes do not move it.

questions.question: Which statistic is the value that occurs most frequently in a dataset?

explanations.A: Correct. The mode is the most frequently occurring value.
explanations.B: The mean is the average; it need not appear in the data at all.
explanations.C: The median is the middle of the sorted data, regardless of frequency.
explanations.D: The range is a spread measure (max − min), unrelated to frequency.

questions.question: A dataset can have two or more of which measure (making it 'bimodal' or 'multimodal')?

explanations.A: A dataset has exactly one arithmetic mean.
explanations.B: Correct. Several values can tie for highest frequency, so a dataset can have multiple modes.
explanations.C: A dataset has a single median for a given ordering.
explanations.D: The range is one number (max − min), not multiple values.

questions.question: Which measure of spread equals the maximum value minus the minimum value?

explanations.A: Variance averages squared deviations from the mean, not max − min.
explanations.B: The median is a measure of center, not spread.
explanations.C: Correct. Range = max − min, the total spread between the extremes.
explanations.D: The IQR is Q3 − Q1 (middle 50%), not the full max − min.

questions.question: Which spread measure is most sensitive to a single extreme value because it uses only the two most extreme points?

explanations.A: The median is a robust center measure, not a spread measure, and resists extremes.
explanations.B: The IQR uses the middle 50% and is robust to extreme values.
explanations.C: The mode reflects frequency, not the extreme values.
explanations.D: Correct. The range depends solely on the max and min, so one extreme value changes it directly.

questions.question: Variance is best described as the average of which quantity?
options: The squared deviations from the mean
The absolute deviations from the mean
The raw deviations from the mean

explanations.A: Correct. Variance = average of (value − mean)², so it averages squared deviations.
explanations.B: Averaging absolute deviations gives the mean absolute deviation, not the variance.
explanations.C: Raw deviations sum to zero, so their average is always 0.
explanations.D: Averaging the values themselves gives the mean, not the variance.

questions.question: If every value in a dataset is identical, what is the variance?

explanations.A: 1 is an arbitrary number with no special role; identical values give exactly 0 spread.
explanations.B: Correct. With no deviation from the mean, every squared deviation is 0, so the variance is 0.
explanations.C: The shared value is the mean, but the spread around it is 0.
explanations.D: Variance is well defined here and equals 0.

questions.question: How is the standard deviation related to the variance?
options: It is the square of the variance
It is the square root of the variance
It is the variance divided by n

explanations.A: Squaring the variance over-inflates units; the SD is the square root.
explanations.B: They are equal only when the variance is 0 or 1; in general SD = √variance.
explanations.C: Correct. Standard deviation = √variance, returning the spread to the original units.
explanations.D: Dividing variance by n is not how SD is defined; SD = √variance.

questions.question: Why is standard deviation often reported instead of variance?
options: It is always smaller than the variance
It is expressed in the same units as the original data

explanations.A: SD is smaller than variance only when variance > 1; that is not the reason it is preferred.
explanations.B: SD, like variance, is sensitive to outliers; it does not ignore them.
explanations.C: SD measures spread and is unrelated to equalling the mean.
explanations.D: Correct. Variance is in squared units; taking the square root returns SD to the data's units, making it interpretable.

questions.question: The 50th percentile of a dataset is also known as the:

explanations.A: Correct. The 50th percentile splits the data in half, which is exactly the median.
explanations.B: The mean is the average and need not sit at the 50th percentile, especially when skewed.
explanations.C: The mode is the most frequent value, unrelated to the 50% split.
explanations.D: The maximum is the 100th percentile, not the 50th.

questions.question: On a histogram, what does the height of a bar represent?
options: The magnitude of the values in that interval
The number of values that fall in that interval

explanations.A: Magnitude is shown on the horizontal axis; height shows counts.
explanations.B: Correct. Bar height encodes frequency: how many data values land in that bin.
explanations.C: Bar height is a frequency, not a single summary statistic.
explanations.D: The range is one number, not the height of each bar.

questions.question: The tallest bar in a histogram corresponds to which statistic?

explanations.A: The mean is a balance point and need not align with the tallest bar.
explanations.B: The median is the 50% split point and need not be the tallest bar.
explanations.C: Correct. The tallest bar is the most frequent bin, i.e., the modal interval.
explanations.D: The range is the width between extremes, not the tallest bar.

questions.question: A histogram with two clearly separated tall peaks is described as:

explanations.A: Symmetry refers to mirror-image balance, not the number of peaks.
explanations.B: A uniform histogram is flat with no peaks.
explanations.C: Right skew describes a single hump with a long right tail, not two peaks.
explanations.D: Correct. Two distinct frequency peaks indicate a bimodal distribution.

questions.question: A histogram where most values sit on the right with a long tail stretching toward small values is:

explanations.A: Correct. The long tail points toward smaller (left) values, so the distribution is left-skewed.
explanations.B: Right skew has the long tail toward larger values, on the right.
explanations.C: Symmetric distributions have balanced tails on both sides.
explanations.D: Uniform distributions are flat with no tail.

questions.question: A flat histogram in which all intervals have roughly equal height is called:

explanations.A: Skewed distributions have a long tail on one side, not a flat shape.
explanations.B: Correct. Roughly equal frequencies across all bins describe a uniform distribution.
explanations.C: A bell shape peaks in the middle rather than being flat.
explanations.D: Bimodal data has two peaks, not a flat profile.

questions.question: A balanced, bell-shaped histogram that looks about the same on both sides is best described as:

explanations.A: Right skew has a long right tail, breaking the symmetry.
explanations.B: Left skew has a long left tail, breaking the symmetry.
explanations.C: Correct. A balanced bell shape with mirror-image tails is symmetric, the hallmark of a normal-like distribution.
explanations.D: Uniform distributions are flat, not bell-shaped.

questions.question: Most values cluster at low numbers with a long tail extending toward large values. This shape is:

explanations.A: Left skew has the long tail toward smaller values.
explanations.B: Symmetric distributions have balanced tails, not one long tail.
explanations.C: Uniform distributions are flat with no tail.
explanations.D: Correct. The long tail points toward larger (right) values, so the distribution is right-skewed.

questions.question: In a right-skewed distribution, how do the mean and median typically compare?

explanations.A: Correct. The long upper tail pulls the mean above the median in right-skewed data.
explanations.B: Mean below median describes left skew, not right skew.
explanations.C: Mean and median are about equal for symmetric data, not skewed data.
explanations.D: They are related; skew direction predicts which is larger.

questions.question: In a left-skewed distribution, how do the mean and median typically compare?

explanations.A: Mean above median describes right skew, not left skew.
explanations.B: Correct. The long lower tail drags the mean below the median in left-skewed data.
explanations.C: Approximate equality describes symmetric data, not skewed data.
explanations.D: They are equal mainly for symmetric distributions, not skewed ones.

questions.question: For an approximately symmetric distribution, how do the mean and median compare?
options: Mean is much greater than median
Mean is much less than median

explanations.A: A large gap with mean above suggests right skew, not symmetry.
explanations.B: A large gap with mean below suggests left skew, not symmetry.
explanations.C: Correct. With balanced tails there is no pull in either direction, so the mean and median are about equal.
explanations.D: The median always exists for a finite dataset.

questions.question: For strongly right-skewed income data, which measure better reflects a 'typical' value?

explanations.A: The mean is pulled upward by the high-income tail, overstating the typical value.
explanations.B: The range measures spread, not a typical central value.
explanations.C: The maximum is an extreme, not a typical value.
explanations.D: Correct. The median resists the long upper tail, while the mean is inflated by a few very large incomes.

questions.question: Which measure of center is MOST affected by a single extreme outlier?

explanations.A: Correct. The mean sums every value, so one extreme value pulls it strongly toward itself.
explanations.B: The median is rank-based and barely moves when one extreme value is added.
explanations.C: The mode only changes if the outlier becomes the most frequent value.
explanations.D: The mean clearly moves, so at least one measure is affected.

questions.question: Which measure of center is LEAST affected by a single extreme outlier?

explanations.A: The mean uses every value, so an outlier pulls it the most.
explanations.B: Correct. The median depends on rank, not magnitude, so one extreme value barely shifts it.
explanations.C: The range is defined by the extremes, so an outlier changes it directly.
explanations.D: If the outlier is large, it becomes the maximum, which is maximally affected.

questions.question: Which measure of spread is most distorted by a single extreme outlier?

explanations.A: The IQR uses the middle 50% and is robust to extreme values.
explanations.B: The median is a center measure, not a spread measure, and resists outliers.
explanations.C: Correct. The range is max − min, so one extreme value changes it directly and dramatically.
explanations.D: The mode reflects frequency and is unaffected by a lone extreme value.

questions.question: Which spread measure is robust to outliers because it ignores the most extreme 25% on each end?

explanations.A: The range is determined entirely by the two most extreme points.
explanations.B: Variance squares deviations, so extreme values dominate it.
explanations.C: SD is the square root of variance and is likewise inflated by outliers.
explanations.D: Correct. The IQR = Q3 − Q1 covers only the middle 50%, so the extreme tails do not affect it.
